name: Notus
description: Notus, in Greek mythology, is the personification of the south wind. He is typically depicted as a winged, bearded man, often with a somber or melancholic expression, reflecting the humid, stormy weather associated with the south wind. Unlike his brothers, Boreas (North), Zephyrus (West), and Eurus (East), Notus is often linked to the late summer and early autumn, bringing with him the heavy rains and mists that can spoil the harvest. He is considered a bringer of storms and is sometimes associated with the hot, dry Sirocco wind that blows north from the Sahara Desert, further illustrating the duality of his nature. Notus is less frequently featured in major mythological narratives compared to his siblings, but his influence on weather patterns and agriculture made him a significant figure in the daily lives of the ancient Greeks. His presence was both feared and respected, as the south wind could bring vital rainfall but also destructive storms. He is a primordial deity, representing a fundamental force of nature, and his impact on the Mediterranean climate was undeniable.
subtitle: The South Wind

keyMyths.title: Notus and the Spoiled Harvest
keyMyths.description: Farmers would pray to Notus, hoping he would bring gentle rains for their crops, but feared his wrath if he unleashed a torrent. One myth tells of a village that neglected to honor Notus properly, leading to a relentless, humid gale that ruined their entire harvest. The villagers learned a harsh lesson about respecting the powers of the wind and the delicate balance between life-giving rain and destructive storms. They then built a shrine to appease him and were rewarded with more controlled weather.

keyMyths.title: Notus and the Lost Sailors
keyMyths.description: Sailors dreaded the sudden squalls brought by Notus. Many tales recount ships lost at sea due to the unpredictable nature of the south wind. One such story describes a fleet returning from a trading expedition that was caught in a violent storm unleashed by Notus. Only a few ships survived, a testament to the raw power of the south wind and the perils of seafaring in the ancient world. The survivors made offerings to Poseidon and Notus for safe passage.

keyMyths.title: Notus and the Drought
keyMyths.description: In a region plagued by drought, the people pleaded with Zeus to send rain. Zeus, in his mercy, commanded Notus to unleash his power. However, Notus, in his eagerness, brought forth torrential downpours that caused floods and erosion. This myth illustrates the idea that even benevolent forces can cause harm if not properly controlled, highlighting the ancient Greek understanding of the delicate balance of nature and the need for moderation.

sources.author: Hesiod
sources.description: Provides a foundational account of the Greek gods and their origins, including the Anemoi (wind gods).
sources.text: Provides a foundational account of the Greek gods and their origins, including the Anemoi (wind gods).

sources.description: A comprehensive collection of Greek myths, offering interpretations and variations of stories involving Notus.

associations.name: Water
associations.significance: Represents the rain and storms brought by the south wind.

associations.name: Autumnal Equinox
associations.significance: Associated with the transition from summer to autumn, marking a shift in weather patterns.

associations.name: Humidity
associations.significance: The moist, damp air characteristic of the south wind's influence.

associations.name: Dark Blue
associations.significance: Symbolizes the stormy seas and rain clouds associated with Notus.

associations.name: Crane
associations.significance: The crane's migration south heralded the arrival of autumn and Notus's winds.

associations.name: Fertility
associations.significance: The rains brought by Notus were essential for agricultural fertility and crop growth.

worshipPractices.name: Libations
worshipPractices.description: Offerings of wine, honey, or water were poured out to appease Notus and ensure favorable winds for agriculture and seafaring.

worshipPractices.name: Sacrifices
worshipPractices.description: In times of drought or severe storms, sacrifices of animals or crops might be made to Notus to implore him for mercy and beneficial weather.

festivals.name: Ruralia
festivals.description: A Roman festival honoring rural deities, including those associated with weather and agriculture, where offerings may have been made to appease winds like Notus for a bountiful harvest.

festivals.name: Vendimia
festivals.description: A festival celebrated in Greece that coincided with the grape harvest. Prayers and offerings would be given to ensure the south winds, including Notus, did not ruin the harvest with excessive storms.

cultural.modernLegacy: Notus's legacy persists in meteorological terminology and artistic depictions of wind deities. He is a recurring figure in literature and art, symbolizing the power and unpredictability of the southern winds.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: In Greek mythology, Notus is one of the four directional wind gods (Anemoi), each representing a cardinal direction. He is the embodiment of the warm, moist south wind. His parentage varies depending on the source; some accounts name Astraeus (god of dusk) and Eos (goddess of dawn) as his parents, linking him to the celestial cycle. Other accounts identify him as a son of the Titans, specifically, of either Perses and Asteria, or of Crius and Eurybia, thus making him a primordial force of nature. This Titan connection emphasizes his powerful and often destructive nature. Unlike some of his more clearly defined siblings, like Boreas (the cold north wind), Notus's character is more ambiguous, reflecting the varied weather patterns associated with the south wind. He is usually depicted as a winged, bearded man, often carrying an urn filled with water or moisture, symbolizing the rain he brings. His appearance is often somber, hinting at the storms and humidity he carries.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: While Notus does not feature prominently in many major myths compared to figures like Zeus or Poseidon, his influence is subtly woven into various narratives. He is often mentioned in the context of weather events, particularly storms and periods of heavy rain. Sailors and farmers would be acutely aware of Notus's power, as his winds could either bring beneficial rain for crops or destructive storms that threatened ships at sea. In some accounts, Notus is associated with the arrival of autumn, bringing with him the end of the summer heat and the beginning of the harvest season. He is also sometimes depicted as a bringer of fog and mist, obscuring the landscape and creating an atmosphere of mystery. Though not a central character, Notus's role as a natural force is crucial to the unfolding of many mythological events, shaping the world and influencing the lives of mortals and gods alike. He is often invoked or placated in rituals aimed at controlling the weather.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: Unlike some of the more prominent Olympian gods, Notus did not have a widespread or formalized cult following with dedicated temples or elaborate rituals. However, he was acknowledged and propitiated as a vital force of nature, particularly in regions heavily dependent on agriculture and seafaring. Farmers would offer libations and prayers to Notus, hoping for gentle rains to nourish their crops and prevent droughts. Sailors would seek his favor for safe passage across the seas, praying for calm winds and protection from storms. Small shrines or altars may have been erected in rural areas, dedicated to the Anemoi collectively, including Notus. These sites would serve as focal points for offerings and appeals to the wind gods. The Romans, heavily influenced by Greek mythology, also recognized Notus under the name Auster. While specific rituals dedicated solely to Notus are scarce in historical records, his importance as a weather deity was undoubtedly acknowledged and integrated into broader religious practices related to agriculture and maritime activities.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Notus's legacy continues to resonate in various aspects of modern culture and scientific understanding. His name is still used in meteorological contexts to refer to the south wind. Artistic representations of Notus, often depicted as a winged, bearded man pouring water from an urn, continue to appear in literature, art, and popular media. He serves as a symbol of the power and unpredictability of nature, reminding us of the forces that shape our world. In literature, Notus frequently appears as a personification of the south wind, contributing to the atmosphere and influencing the narrative. His character embodies the ambiguity of the south wind, capable of bringing both life-giving rain and destructive storms. Furthermore, Notus's association with autumn and the transition of seasons has made him a recurring motif in poetry and art, representing themes of change, decay, and renewal. His enduring presence in various forms of media underscores his lasting impact on human imagination and cultural understanding of the natural world.

shortDescription: Notus is the Greek god personifying the south wind, bringing storms and rain, often associated with late summer and autumn.
